content.text: What It Is
Meta-prompting is the technique of using a language model to write, critique, or improve
another prompt
— or to write the prompt that a different model or pipeline step will receive. Instead of a human composing instructions from scratch, you describe the task to a
meta-LLM
(which may be the same model) and ask it to act as a skilled prompt engineer on your behalf.

The meta-prompt pattern separates two concerns: (1) what you want to accomplish and (2) how to communicate that goal to an LLM effectively. You own the first; the meta-LLM handles the second. The output of the meta-LLM is a prompt — a string you then feed to a target LLM to perform the actual task.
Meta-prompting encompasses several related techniques:
Prompt generation
: describe a task; the meta-LLM produces a system prompt for it from scratch
Prompt improvement
: supply an existing prompt; the meta-LLM rewrites it to be clearer, more concise, or better aligned with a style guide
Prompt translation
: convert a prompt optimized for one model (GPT-4) into an equivalent for another (Claude) — adapting tone, formatting, and model-specific idioms
Automatic chain-of-thought generation
: ask the meta-LLM to generate diverse few-shot reasoning examples for a task, rather than writing them by hand
Prompt expansion
: start with a one-line task description; the meta-LLM produces a full, detailed system prompt with persona, constraints, and format instructions
Why It Matters

Writing effective prompts is a craft. A well-structured system prompt with the right persona, explicit output constraints, and carefully chosen examples can double task accuracy compared to a naive instruction. But crafting that prompt requires expertise, time, and repeated experimentation. Meta-prompting transfers that craft work to the model itself.
At scale, meta-prompting addresses three painful problems:
Bootstrapping new tasks
: when you need a prompt for a novel domain (legal clause extraction, sensor-data anomaly narration), a meta-LLM can produce a solid first draft in seconds, which a human then reviews and refines
Model migration
: a prompt tuned for GPT-4 often degrades when moved to Claude or Gemini because of stylistic and formatting differences; the meta-LLM can translate between idioms
Diverse example generation
: manually writing 20 few-shot examples is tedious; the meta-LLM can generate varied, on-distribution examples given a handful of seeds

Meta-prompting is also the conceptual foundation for automated prompt optimization frameworks like DSPy and OPRO, which treat the prompt itself as a learnable parameter — turning prompt engineering into a structured search problem rather than a manual art.
Benefits
Fast bootstrapping: go from task description to working prompt in seconds rather than hours
Broader search: a meta-LLM explores a larger space of instructional framings than a single human author
Model-aware translation: the meta-LLM knows about model-specific idioms and can adapt prompts accordingly
Automatic few-shot generation: produce diverse, on-distribution examples without writing each one by hand
Prompt quality as a metric: meta-prompting pipelines can be coupled to evaluators, enabling iterative refinement toward a measurable goal
Reduced expert bottleneck: junior engineers can get a quality prompt for a new task without waiting for a prompt expert
Exploration of constraint variations: quickly generate multiple prompt variants with different personas, verbosity levels, or output formats for comparative evaluation
Trade-offs
Meta-prompting is powerful but introduces its own failure modes:
Circular dependency on prompt quality
: the quality of the generated prompt depends on the quality of the meta-prompt. If the meta-prompt is vague, the output will be too.
No ground-truth signal by default
: the meta-LLM has no way to know if its generated prompt actually produces good outputs unless you close the loop with an evaluator
Plausible-sounding but wrong
: generated prompts can look polished while missing domain-critical constraints that a human expert would know to include
Instruction drift
: iterative meta-prompting can drift away from the original intent if feedback is too broad — each refinement iteration should be anchored to a specific failing example
Cost
: running a meta-LLM call before every target LLM call doubles cost at minimum; meta-prompting should be an offline or infrequent online step
Non-determinism
: the same meta-prompt produces different outputs across runs; always evaluate candidates rather than assuming the first output is the best
How It Works
The core meta-prompting pattern requires three inputs:
Task description
: what the target LLM must do (e.g., "extract medical entities from clinical notes")
Context / constraints
: output format, tone, length, audience, edge cases to handle
Optional seed examples
: one or two input/output pairs that illustrate the desired behavior

These inputs form the meta-prompt. The meta-LLM responds with a prompt — typically a system prompt, sometimes a full prompt with few-shot examples. You then evaluate that prompt against a held-out example set, score the outputs, and optionally feed the score back to the meta-LLM with a request to refine. This constitutes the
generate → evaluate → refine
loop.

The number of refinement iterations depends on the target score: stop when the prompt crosses a quality threshold or after a fixed budget of iterations. In offline optimization pipelines (DSPy, OPRO), this loop runs automatically until convergence.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the full meta-prompting loop: the task description enters the meta-LLM, which produces a candidate prompt. The target LLM runs that prompt on an evaluation set. An evaluator scores the outputs and either accepts the prompt (Done) or feeds the score back to the meta-LLM for another iteration.

attrs.alt: Meta-prompting refinement loop: Task Description → Meta-LLM → Generated Prompt → Target LLM → Output → Evaluator → decision gate feeding back to Meta-LLM

content.text: When to Use It
Meta-prompting is most valuable in these situations:
You need a first-draft prompt for a new task and have no domain expert available
You are migrating prompts from one model family to another (e.g., GPT-4 to Claude)
You need to generate a large set of diverse few-shot examples for a task
You have an evaluation suite and want to run automated prompt optimization
You are building a system that adapts its prompts based on task metadata (multi-tenant systems where each tenant has a different task specification)
You want to explore multiple instructional framings quickly before committing to one

Avoid it when: the task is already well-prompted and performing adequately (the cost of running the meta-LLM is not justified), when the domain requires deep expert knowledge the meta-LLM does not possess, or when you lack an evaluation mechanism (you need feedback to close the loop).
How to Implement
Basic Meta-Prompt: Ask the Model to Write a System Prompt

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

META_PROMPT = """You are an expert prompt engineer.

Your job: write a high-quality system prompt for the following task.

Task description: {task_description}

Target audience: {audience}

Output format required: {output_format}

Constraints:
{constraints}

Write the system prompt below. Start directly with the prompt content — no preamble."""

def generate_system_prompt(
    task_description: str,
    audience: str = "a general-purpose language model",
    output_format: str = "free-form text",
    constraints: list[str] | None = None,
) -> str:
    constraints_str = "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in (constraints or []))
    meta_prompt = META_PROMPT.format(
        task_description=task_description,
        audience=audience,
        output_format=output_format,
        constraints=constraints_str or "None specified",
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": meta_prompt}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Example: generate a prompt for legal clause classification
system_prompt = generate_system_prompt(
    task_description="Classify contract clauses into one of: liability, indemnification, IP assignment, termination, confidentiality",
    output_format="JSON: {\"label\": \"...\", \"confidence\": 0.0-1.0}",
    constraints=[
        "Classify exactly one label per clause",
        "If uncertain, output confidence below 0.5",
        "Do not infer intent beyond the literal text",
    ],
)
print(system_prompt)
Iterative Meta-Prompting: Generate → Evaluate → Improve

import json
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

IMPROVE_PROMPT = """You are an expert prompt engineer.

Here is the current system prompt:
<current_prompt>
{current_prompt}
</current_prompt>

Here is a failing example (input and actual vs. expected output):
Input: {input_example}
Expected: {expected}
Actual: {actual}

Rewrite the system prompt to fix this failure. Output only the improved prompt."""

def evaluate_prompt(system_prompt: str, test_cases: list[dict]) -> float:
    """Returns accuracy on test_cases: [{input, expected}]"""
    correct = 0
    for case in test_cases:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5",
            max_tokens=256,
            system=system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}],
        )
        actual = response.content[0].text.strip()
        if case["expected"].lower() in actual.lower():
            correct += 1
    return correct / len(test_cases)

def meta_prompt_loop(
    initial_prompt: str,
    test_cases: list[dict],
    max_iterations: int = 5,
    target_score: float = 0.9,
) -> tuple[str, float]:
    current_prompt = initial_prompt
    for i in range(max_iterations):
        score = evaluate_prompt(current_prompt, test_cases)
        print(f"Iteration {i}: score={score:.2f}")
        if score >= target_score:
            print("Target reached — stopping.")
            break
        # Find a failing example
        failing = next(
            (c for c in test_cases
             if c["expected"].lower() not in
                client.messages.create(
                    model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=256,
                    system=current_prompt,
                    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": c["input"]}]
                ).content[0].text.lower()),
            test_cases[0]
        )
        actual = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=256,
            system=current_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": failing["input"]}]
        ).content[0].text.strip()
        # Ask meta-LLM to improve
        improve_prompt = IMPROVE_PROMPT.format(
            current_prompt=current_prompt,
            input_example=failing["input"],
            expected=failing["expected"],
            actual=actual,
        )
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": improve_prompt}],
        )
        current_prompt = response.content[0].text.strip()
    return current_prompt, evaluate_prompt(current_prompt, test_cases)
Few-Shot Example Generation via Meta-Prompting
Prompt Translation: GPT-4 Style to Claude Style

TRANSLATE_PROMPT = """You are adapting a prompt written for GPT-4 to work optimally with Claude.

Key differences to apply:
- Claude responds better to conversational, direct tone than to formal instructions
- Replace "You must" with "Please" or "Your job is to"
- Claude handles XML tags well for structured input; use <input>...</input> blocks
- Remove OpenAI-specific system-prompt idioms (references to "As an AI", "I am ChatGPT")
- Claude does not need explicit "Think step by step" — use "Walk through your reasoning" if needed
- Preserve all task-specific constraints exactly

Original prompt (written for GPT-4):
{original_prompt}

Output the adapted Claude prompt. Start directly with the prompt text."""

def translate_to_claude(gpt4_prompt: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": TRANSLATE_PROMPT.format(
            original_prompt=gpt4_prompt
        )}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip()
Examples
A concrete run of the iterative loop:
Starting with a naive prompt
"Classify the intent of this customer message."

, the meta-LLM first expands it into a structured system prompt with explicit label definitions, output format constraints, and handling instructions for ambiguous cases. After one evaluation pass at 0.62 accuracy, the evaluator surfaces a failing example (a message that spans two intents), and the meta-LLM adds a tiebreaker rule. After three iterations the prompt reaches 0.91 accuracy — comparable to what a human prompt engineer might achieve after a week of iteration.
Real-World Example

The Automatic Prompt Engineer (APE) paper (Zhou et al., 2022) demonstrated meta-prompting on the BIG-Bench benchmark. The meta-LLM was shown a handful of input/output examples and asked to generate candidate instructions. Those instructions were scored on a dev set and the top-scoring one was selected. On several tasks, APE-generated prompts
matched or outperformed
human-written prompts — sometimes because the meta-LLM found non-obvious framings that humans had not considered.

DSPy (Khattab et al., 2023) operationalizes this loop as a compiled optimization: prompts are replaced by typed signatures, the optimizer (BootstrapFewShot or MIPROv2) generates few-shot examples and instructions through meta-prompting, and the compiled program is checkpointed as a reproducible artifact. Teams at Stanford and various industry labs have used DSPy to reduce manual prompt engineering effort by 60–80% on complex multi-step pipelines.
Common Mistakes
Trusting the first output
: the first generated prompt is a starting point, not a finished product. Always evaluate against real examples before deploying.
Vague meta-prompt
: "write a good prompt for my task" produces generic output. Specify the output format, audience, constraints, and provide examples in the meta-prompt.
No evaluation set
: without labeled examples to score against, the meta-prompting loop has no feedback signal and cannot improve systematically.
Using meta-prompting at inference time
: calling a meta-LLM on every user request doubles latency and cost. Meta-prompting is an offline optimization step; the generated prompt is deployed statically.
Ignoring domain constraints
: the meta-LLM does not know your business rules, regulatory requirements, or brand voice. Always review generated prompts for domain-specific correctness.
Over-iterating on poor seeds
: if the evaluation set is too small or biased, the loop will overfit to it. Use at least 50 diverse examples; reserve a held-out test set for final assessment.
Best Practices
Write a detailed meta-prompt: include task description, output format, target audience, explicit constraints, and 1-2 seed examples
Always close the loop with evaluation: generate → score → refine; never deploy on the first output
Keep meta-prompting offline: run the optimization before deployment, not at inference time
Version the output: the generated prompt is a prompt artifact — store it under version control with the meta-prompt that produced it for reproducibility
Review for domain correctness: have a domain expert inspect the generated prompt before deploying, especially for regulated domains
Use diverse evaluation sets: at least 50 examples covering edge cases, not just the happy path
Cap iteration budget: set a maximum of 5-10 refinement iterations to avoid runaway cost and diminishing returns
Preserve the meta-prompt: record the meta-prompt alongside the generated prompt so you can reproduce or extend the optimization later
Performance & Scalability
Meta-prompting is almost always an offline process, so raw latency matters less than quality and cost:
Meta-LLM selection
: use the best available model (e.g., Claude Opus or GPT-4o) for generation; it pays dividends in prompt quality. Use a faster/cheaper model (Haiku, GPT-4o-mini) for evaluation.
Parallelism
: generate multiple candidate prompts in one meta-LLM call (ask for 3 variants), evaluate them in parallel, and select the best — this often yields better results than serial iteration.
Caching
: generated prompts should be committed to a registry and cached. Do not re-run meta-prompting on every deployment unless the task spec changed.
Eval set size
: 50-100 examples is sufficient for most tasks. Larger sets give more reliable signal but increase cost; use stratified sampling to cover the output distribution.
Token budget
: meta-LLM inputs include the full meta-prompt plus possibly the current prompt and a failing example. Keep meta-prompt inputs under 2K tokens to avoid diluting attention on the instruction.
Tooling & Ecosystem
DSPy
: Python framework that replaces manual prompt writing with typed signatures; the MIPROv2 optimizer uses meta-prompting internally to generate instructions and few-shot examples
OPRO (Google DeepMind)
: Optimization by PROmpting — provides the meta-LLM with a history of previous candidates and their scores, asking it to propose a better one; effectively textual gradient descent
APE (Automatic Prompt Engineer)
: research framework from Zhou et al. that samples prompt candidates from a meta-LLM and selects by likelihood or accuracy
LangChain FewShotPromptTemplate
: supports dynamic few-shot example selection; pairs with meta-prompting for example generation
Anthropic prompt improvement tool
: Claude's built-in capability — you can ask Claude directly to improve a prompt, making every Claude session a lightweight meta-prompting environment
Promptfoo
: prompt evaluation framework that can be integrated as the evaluator in a meta-prompting loop
Braintrust
: evaluation and experiment tracking platform that can score candidate prompts across a dataset, suitable for the evaluate step in the loop

Security & Reliability
Meta-prompting introduces risks that pure manual prompting does not:
Prompt injection via task description
: if the task description fed to the meta-LLM contains adversarial text (e.g., from a user), it may hijack the generated prompt. Always sanitize task descriptions before embedding in a meta-prompt.
Bias amplification
: the meta-LLM may encode biases from its training data into the generated prompt (e.g., stereotyped personas or loaded framing). Review generated prompts for fairness before deployment.
Overconfident constraints
: the meta-LLM may generate plausible-sounding but incorrect constraints ("always respond in ISO 8601 date format" when no date is involved). Domain review catches these.
Reproducibility

: non-deterministic generation means the same meta-prompt may produce different prompts across runs. Commit the generated prompt to version control and record the meta-prompt and model version used to produce it.
Eval set contamination
: if examples from the evaluation set are inadvertently included in the meta-prompt context, the loop will overfit. Keep the eval set separate from the meta-prompt inputs.
Cascading failures
: in a multi-step pipeline where each step's prompt was generated by meta-prompting, a poor meta-prompt for one step propagates errors downstream. Test each step's prompt independently.
Mental Model
Think of meta-prompting as
hiring a professional technical writer to draft your instructions

. You describe the problem and what you need communicated, and they craft the communication strategy. You do not expect their first draft to be perfect — you review it, identify where it fails, give feedback, and iterate.
The key insight is that
generating a good prompt
and
executing a task

are different cognitive activities. Humans are often better at the former when they understand their domain; LLMs are often better at the latter in terms of scale and consistency. Meta-prompting lets you exploit the LLM's fluency with instructional language while retaining human judgment about domain correctness and quality criteria.

This also explains the most common failure mode: asking the meta-LLM to write a prompt without giving it any examples or evaluation criteria is like asking the technical writer to draft instructions for a product they have never seen. The richer the context you provide in the meta-prompt, the more useful the output.
Key Takeaways
Meta-prompting uses an LLM to write or improve prompts, separating prompt authorship from task execution
The four main variants are: prompt generation, prompt improvement, prompt translation, and few-shot example generation
Quality depends entirely on the meta-prompt: a vague meta-prompt produces a generic output
Always close the loop with evaluation: generate → score on a labeled eval set → refine until quality threshold is met
Meta-prompting is an offline process — run it before deployment, not at inference time
Review generated prompts for domain correctness; the meta-LLM does not know your business rules or regulatory constraints
DSPy and OPRO formalize the generate → evaluate → refine loop as automated optimization, treating the prompt as a learnable parameter
Store the generated prompt in version control alongside the meta-prompt that produced it for reproducibility
The mental model: describe your problem to a skilled technical writer and iterate on their draft with specific failure examples
